title: React Native: The Complete A–Z Guide
description: A comprehensive end-to-end guide to React Native from fundamentals to production. Covers every major concept — core components, styling, flexbox/Yoga, navigation, state management, networking, data persistence, device APIs (camera, location, permissions), animations (Reanimated, Animated API), gestures, forms, debugging, testing, push notifications, performance optimization, TypeScript, publishing to app stores, security, CI/CD, and production best practices — for beginners and experienced developers alike.
author: E-Learning Platform
heroAlt: React Native architecture diagram showing the bridge, core components, navigation, state management, and deployment pipeline

sections.title: Introduction to React Native
sections.description: What React Native is, how it works, and why it has become the leading framework for cross-platform mobile development.

items.label: What is React Native?
items.text:
React Native is an open-source mobile application framework created by Meta (formerly Facebook). It lets you build native mobile applications for iOS and Android using a single JavaScript/TypeScript codebase. Unlike hybrid approaches (Cordova, Ionic) that render web views, React Native compiles to real native UI components.

The core principle: "Learn once, write anywhere." You write React components in JavaScript/TypeScript, and React Native renders them using native platform UI primitives — UIView on iOS, android.view on Android.

Key milestones:
- 2015: React Native open-sourced by Meta
- 2019: React Native 0.60 (Auto-linking, CocoaPods by default)
- 2020: React Native 0.63 (LogBox, Pressable)
- 2021: React Native 0.64 (Hermes as default on Android)
- 2022: React Native 0.70 (New Architecture — JSI + Fabric + TurboModules)
- 2023: React Native 0.73 (New Architecture stable, Yoga 3.0)
- 2024: React Native 0.75 (New Architecture default, Bridgeless mode)
- 2025+: React Native 0.78+ (Fabric renderer everywhere, synchronous JSI)

items.label: Why React Native?
text: Cross-Platform — Write once, ship to iOS and Android with 90%+ code sharing.
Native Performance — Renders real native UI components, not web views.
Hot Reloading / Fast Refresh — See changes instantly without recompiling.
Huge Ecosystem — Thousands of libraries via npm + Native Modules.
Large Community — Backed by Meta, Expo, Microsoft, Shopify, Coinbase.
TypeScript Support — First-class TypeScript support.
Over-the-Air Updates — Use CodePush or EAS Update.
Existing React Skills — If you know React, you know most of React Native.

items.label: Architecture: The Bridge vs The New Architecture
items.text:
React Native has two architectures:

Old Architecture (Bridge):
- JavaScript thread and Native thread communicate via an asynchronous JSON serialization bridge
- Batched messages — every async call creates overhead
- Type conversions (JS -> JSON -> Native) cost performance
- Limited by bridge serialization speed

New Architecture (JSI + Fabric + TurboModules):
- JSI (JavaScript Interface): Direct C++ binding between JS and Native — no JSON serialization
- Fabric: Synchronous rendering — UI updates in the same frame
- TurboModules: Lazy-loaded native modules — only load when needed
- Codegen: TypeScript props flow to C++ type safety
- Bridgeless mode: No bridge at all in RN 0.75+

Migration to New Architecture:
- Since RN 0.75+, it is the default for new projects
- Use newArchEnabled: true in react-native.config.js for existing projects
- All major libraries (React Navigation, Reanimated, Gesture Handler) support it

sections.title: Setup & Environment Configuration
sections.description: Setting up your development environment for React Native — from Expo to bare workflow.

items.label: Prerequisites
items.text:
Before starting, ensure you have:

- Node.js 18 LTS or newer (recommended: 22 LTS)
- A code editor (VS Code with React Native Tools extension recommended)
- For iOS: macOS (Xcode 15+), CocoaPods (sudo gem install cocoapods)
- For Android: Android Studio, JDK 17+, Android SDK (API 34+)
- Basic knowledge of React (components, props, state, hooks)

items.label: Option 1: Expo (Recommended for Beginners)
items.code:
# Create a new Expo project (managed workflow):
user@dev:~$ npx create-expo-app@latest MyReactNativeApp

# Navigate to project:
user@dev:~$ cd MyReactNativeApp

# Start the dev server:
user@dev:~$ npx expo start

# This opens Expo Developer Tools in browser.
# Scan QR code with Expo Go app on your phone.
# Or press 'a' for Android emulator / 'i' for iOS simulator.

# Expo project structure:
MyReactNativeApp/
  app.json              # Expo configuration
  App.js                # Entry component
  assets/               # Images, fonts, etc.
  package.json
  node_modules/

# Key Expo commands:
user@dev:~$ npx expo start          # Start dev server
user@dev:~$ npx expo start --tunnel  # Share over internet
user@dev:~$ npx expo build           # Build for stores
user@dev:~$ npx expo run:android     # Bare Android run
user@dev:~$ npx expo run:ios         # Bare iOS run
user@dev:~$ npx expo eject           # Eject to bare workflow

items.label: Option 2: React Native CLI (Bare Workflow)
items.code:
# Create a new React Native project (bare workflow):
user@dev:~$ npx @react-native-community/cli init MyReactNativeApp

# Or with TypeScript template:
user@dev:~$ npx @react-native-community/cli init MyReactNativeApp --template react-native-template-typescript

# Project structure:
MyReactNativeApp/
  android/              # Android native project (Gradle)
  ios/                  # iOS native project (Xcode)
  App.tsx               # Entry component
  index.js              # App registry
  metro.config.js       # Metro bundler config
  babel.config.js       # Babel config
  tsconfig.json         # TypeScript config
  package.json

# Install iOS dependencies:
user@dev:~$ cd ios && pod install && cd ..

# Run on Android:
user@dev:~$ npx react-native run-android

# Run on iOS:
user@dev:~$ npx react-native run-ios

# Run on specific device/simulator:
user@dev:~$ npx react-native run-ios --simulator="iPhone 16 Pro"
user@dev:~$ npx react-native run-android --deviceId=emulator-5554

items.label: Expo vs React Native CLI: Decision Guide

sections.title: Project Structure Best Practices
sections.description: How to organize your React Native project for scalability, maintainability, and team collaboration.

items.label: Recommended Directory Layout
items.code:
MyReactNativeApp/
  src/
    app/                  # Navigation setup (React Navigation)
    components/           # Reusable UI components
      ui/                 # Design system primitives (Button, Input, Card)
      shared/             # Shared feature components
    features/             # Feature-based modules
      auth/               # Authentication feature
        screens/          # Auth screens
        components/       # Auth-specific components
        hooks/            # Auth-specific hooks
        api.ts            # Auth API calls
        types.ts          # Auth TypeScript types
      home/
      profile/
      settings/
    navigation/           # Navigation configuration
      RootNavigator.tsx
      types.ts            # Navigation param types
    services/             # External services (API, push notifications)
      api.ts              # Axios/fetch instance
      notifications.ts
    store/                # State management
    hooks/                # Global custom hooks
    utils/                # Utility functions
    constants/            # App-wide constants
    types/                # Global TypeScript types
    assets/               # Images, fonts, animations
  __tests__/              # Test files
  App.tsx                 # Root component
  index.js               # Entry point

items.label: Feature-Based Organization
items.text:
Organizing by feature (not by type) is the recommended approach:

By Type (older pattern — avoid):
src/screens/ -> 50+ files, hard to find
src/components/ -> 100+ unrelated components

By Feature (recommended):
src/features/auth/ -> Everything for auth in one place
src/features/home/ -> Everything for home in one place

Benefits:
- Co-location: screen + components + hooks + API are together
- Scoped imports
- Easier refactoring: delete one folder instead of hunting files
- Lazy loading: code-split by feature naturally

sections.title: Core Components — The Building Blocks
sections.description: Every React Native app is built from a set of core components that map to native UI primitives.

items.label: The Essential Components
All Core Components at a Glance

sections.title: Styling in React Native
sections.description: All React Native styling uses JavaScript objects — no CSS files, no selectors, no cascade.

items.label: StyleSheet — The Standard Way

rows: position, top, right, bottom, left, zIndex
width, height, minWidth, maxWidth, minHeight, maxHeight

Using Tailwind CSS (NativeWind)

sections.title: Flexbox Layout — Yoga Engine
sections.description: React Native uses Yoga, a C++ layout engine implementing a subset of CSS Flexbox.

Flexbox Patterns & Recipes

sections.title: Navigation — Moving Between Screens
sections.description: React Navigation is the de facto standard for navigation in React Native apps.

items.label: Installing React Navigation
items.code:
# Install core navigation:
user@dev:~$ npm install @react-navigation/native @react-navigation/native-stack @react-navigation/bottom-tabs @react-navigation/drawer

# Install dependencies:
user@dev:~$ npm install react-native-screens react-native-safe-area-context

# For iOS, install pods:
user@dev:~$ cd ios && pod install && cd ..

# React Navigation versions:
# - @react-navigation/native: Core navigation logic
# - @react-navigation/native-stack: Stack navigator (native, recommended)
# - @react-navigation/bottom-tabs: Bottom tab navigator
# - @react-navigation/drawer: Drawer navigator

items.label: Stack Navigator (Native Stack)
Tab Navigator (Bottom Tabs)
Navigation Best Practices
text: 1. Always type your navigation params with TypeScript.
2. Use useNavigation hook in deep child components — don't pass navigation as a prop.
3. Nest navigators: Tabs -> Stacks -> Screens.
4. Use presentation: 'modal' for modals that slide up from the bottom.
5. Configure deep linking in NavigationContainer for push notifications.
6. Avoid passing complex objects as params — store in state and pass IDs.
7. Use navigation.reset() for authentication flows (login -> main screen).
8. Use navigation.replace() for splash -> main transitions.

sections.title: State Management
sections.description: Managing state in React Native — from local state to global state with modern solutions.

items.label: Local State: useState and useReducer
Global State: Zustand (Recommended)

sections.title: Networking & APIs
sections.description: Communicating with servers, fetching data, handling offline state, and managing API calls.

items.label: Built-in fetch() API
Axios — More Powerful HTTP Client
TanStack Query (React Query) for Server State
Offline-First Strategy
items.text:
React Native apps should work offline. Key strategies:

1. NetInfo — Detect connectivity:
   import NetInfo from '@react-native-community/netinfo';
   const unsubscribe = NetInfo.addEventListener(state => {
     console.log('Is connected:', state.isConnected);
   });

2. AsyncStorage / MMKV — Cache data locally:
   - Store last-fetched data so the app is usable offline
   - Queue mutations and sync when back online

3. Offline-first libraries:
   - TanStack Query (persister)
   - WatermelonDB — SQLite-based local database
   - RxDB — Reactive local database with replication

4. Pattern: Stale-While-Revalidate:
   - Show cached data immediately
   - Fetch fresh data from API
   - Update UI with fresh data
   - Update local cache

5. Mutation Queue:
   - User creates a comment while offline
   - Store the mutation in a queue (AsyncStorage)
   - When network restores, replay queued mutations

sections.title: Data Persistence — Local Storage
sections.description: Storing data locally on the device for offline access, caching, and app state persistence.

items.label: AsyncStorage (Key-Value)
MMKV — High-Performance Alternative

sections.title: Lists & Performance Optimization
sections.description: Rendering large lists efficiently and optimizing overall app performance.

items.label: FlatList — The Virtualized List
Performance Optimization Checklist
text: 1. Use useCallback for renderItem — Prevents re-creating the function on every render.
2. Use useMemo for list headers/footers.
3. Set windowSize lower (5 instead of default 21) for memory-constrained devices.
4. Enable removeClippedSubviews={true} on Android.
5. Keep list items lightweight — no heavy images, avoid deep nesting.
6. Use getItemLayout if all items have the same height — eliminates measurement overhead.
7. Use React.memo on list item components.
8. Enable Hermes — JavaScript engine optimized for React Native.
9. Use InteractionManager.runAfterInteractions() — Defer non-critical work.
10. Profile with React DevTools Profiler and Flipper.
11. Use useNativeDriver: true for animations — runs on native thread.
12. Image optimization — use WebP format, resize server-side, use blurhash placeholders.

items.label: getItemLayout — Fixed Height Items

sections.title: Publishing to App Stores
sections.description: Building, signing, and publishing your React Native app to the Apple App Store and Google Play Store.

items.label: Building for Production
items.code:
# --- Android Release Build ---

# Generate upload key (one-time):
user@dev:~$ keytool -genkey -v -keystore my-upload-key.keystore -alias my-key-alias -keyalg RSA -keysize 2048 -validity 10000

# Build AAB (Android App Bundle — recommended for Play Store):
user@dev:~$ cd android && ./gradlew bundleRelease && cd ..
# Output: android/app/build/outputs/bundle/release/app-release.aab

# Build APK (for direct installation):
user@dev:~$ cd android && ./gradlew assembleRelease && cd ..
# Output: android/app/build/outputs/apk/release/app-release.apk

# --- iOS Release Build ---

# Open in Xcode:
user@dev:~$ open ios/MyApp.xcworkspace

# Steps in Xcode:
# 1. Select Product > Scheme > Edit Scheme
# 2. Set Build Configuration to Release
# 3. Select Product > Archive
# 4. In Organizer, distribute to App Store Connect

# Or build via command line:
user@dev:~$ cd ios && xcodebuild -workspace MyApp.xcworkspace -scheme MyApp -configuration Release archive -archivePath build/MyApp.xcarchive && cd ..

# --- With Expo EAS Build ---
user@dev:~$ npx eas build --platform all  # Build both platforms
user@dev:~$ npx eas submit --platform ios    # Submit to App Store
user@dev:~$ npx eas submit --platform android  # Submit to Play Store
user@dev:~$ npx eas update --branch production --message "Fix login bug"  # OTA update

items.label: App Store Configuration Checklist
text:
iOS App Store:
  1. Apple Developer Program ($99/year) — Enroll at developer.apple.com
  2. Create App ID and Bundle Identifier in Apple Developer Console
  3. Generate App Store Connect API Key for EAS Submit
  4. Configure App Icon (1024x1024) and Screenshots (6.7", 6.5", 5.5" displays)
  5. Set up Certificates, Identifiers & Profiles
  6. Prepare App Store metadata (name, subtitle, keywords, description)
  7. Set up In-App Purchases or Subscriptions if applicable
  8. Submit for Review (24-48 hours typical)

Google Play Store:
  1. Google Play Developer account ($25 one-time)
  2. Create application in Play Console
  3. Generate upload key (keep it safe!)
  4. Configure App signing key
  5. Prepare store listing (graphics, screenshots, description)
  6. Set content rating questionnaire
  7. Set pricing distribution (free or paid)
  8. Roll out production (staged rollout recommended)

Both Stores:
  - App privacy questionnaire (required)
  - Data Safety section (Google Play)
  - App Tracking Transparency (iOS 14.5+)
  - Compliance with local regulations (GDPR, CCPA)

sections.title: TypeScript with React Native
sections.description: Using TypeScript effectively in React Native for type safety and better developer experience.

items.label: TypeScript Configuration
items.code:
// tsconfig.json — Standard React Native TypeScript config
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "target": "esnext",
    "module": "commonjs",
    "lib": ["es2022"],
    "allowJs": true,
    "jsx": "react-native",
    "strict": true,
    "moduleResolution": "node",
    "resolveJsonModule": true,
    "isolatedModules": true,
    "noEmit": true,
    "esModuleInterop": true,
    "skipLibCheck": true,
    "forceConsistentCasingInFileNames": true,
    "baseUrl": ".",
    "paths": {
      "@/*": ["src/*"],
      "@components/*": ["src/components/*"],
      "@features/*": ["src/features/*"],
      "@utils/*": ["src/utils/*"]
    }
  },
  "include": ["src/**/*", "index.js", "App.tsx"],
  "exclude": ["node_modules", "babel.config.js", "metro.config.js"]
}

items.label: Common TypeScript Patterns in React Native

sections.title: Security Best Practices
sections.description: Securing your React Native application and protecting user data.

items.label: Security Checklist
text: 1. Store secrets securely — Never hardcode API keys, tokens, or passwords. Use environment variables via react-native-config or expo-constants.
2. Use encrypted storage — Store sensitive data (tokens, biometrics) in Keychain (iOS) / EncryptedSharedPreferences (Android) via react-native-keychain or MMKV with encryption.
3. Implement certificate pinning — Use react-native-ssl-pinning or axios with custom httpsAgent to prevent MITM attacks.
4. Secure network communication — Always use HTTPS, never HTTP in production. Implement proper TLS configuration.
5. Deep link validation — Validate all incoming deep links to prevent URL scheme hijacking. Use verified links (iOS) / App Links (Android).
6. Input validation — Validate all user input both client-side and server-side. Sanitize inputs to prevent injection attacks.
7. Authentication — Use OAuth 2.0 / OpenID Connect. Implement biometric authentication for sensitive operations. Use short-lived tokens with refresh token rotation.
8. Code obfuscation — Use ProGuard (Android) and build with Release configuration to minimize reverse engineering risk.
9. Jailbreak/root detection — Use react-native-jailbreak-detection to prevent running on compromised devices (for high-security apps).
10. Regular dependency audits — Run `npm audit` regularly. Keep all dependencies updated. Use Dependabot or Snyk for automated vulnerability scanning.
11. App Transport Security (iOS) — Configure Info.plist to restrict network connections to HTTPS only.
12. Network Security Config (Android) — Use network_security_config.xml to enforce HTTPS and certificate pinning.
13. Logging — Never log sensitive information (tokens, passwords, PII). Strip logs in production builds.
14. WebView safety — If using WebView, disable JavaScript unless required, validate URLs, and use allowlist pattern.
15. Data minimization — Only collect and store data that is absolutely necessary. Follow privacy-by-design principles.

sections.title: CI/CD for React Native
sections.description: Setting up continuous integration and deployment pipelines for React Native apps.

items.label: GitHub Actions Workflow
items.code:
# .github/workflows/ci.yml
name: React Native CI

on:
  push:
    branches: [main, develop]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  lint-and-test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 22
          cache: 'npm'

      - run: npm ci

      - run: npx eslint src/

      - run: npx jest --coverage

      - uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        if: always()
        with:
          name: coverage
          path: coverage/

  android-build:
    needs: lint-and-test
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 22
      - run: npm ci

      - name: Setup JDK
        uses: actions/setup-java@v4
        with:
          distribution: 'temurin'
          java-version: '17'

      - name: Build Android Release
        run: cd android && ./gradlew bundleRelease

      - uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: android-release
          path: android/app/build/outputs/bundle/release/app-release.aab

  ios-build:
    needs: lint-and-test
    runs-on: macos-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 22

      - run: npm ci

      - name: Install Pods
        run: cd ios && pod install

      - name: Build iOS
        run: |
          xcodebuild -workspace ios/MyApp.xcworkspace \
            -scheme MyApp \
            -configuration Release \
            -sdk iphoneos \
            -derivedDataPath ios/build \
            archive -archivePath ios/build/MyApp.xcarchive

      - uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: ios-release
          path: ios/build/MyApp.xcarchive

items.label: Fastlane for Deployment Automation
items.code:
# Fastlane automates beta deployments and App Store releases
# Install: sudo gem install fastlane -NV

# fastlane/Fastfile
fastlane_version "2.220.0"

platform :ios do
  desc "Deploy to TestFlight"
  lane :beta do
    increment_build_number
    build_app(scheme: "MyApp", workspace: "ios/MyApp.xcworkspace")
    upload_to_testflight(
      skip_waiting_for_build_processing: true
    )
  end

  desc "Deploy to App Store"
  lane :release do
    match(type: "appstore")
    increment_build_number
    build_app(scheme: "MyApp", workspace: "ios/MyApp.xcworkspace", configuration: "Release")
    upload_to_app_store(
      skip_metadata: true,
      skip_screenshots: true
    )
  end
end

platform :android do
  desc "Deploy to Play Console"
  lane :beta do
    increment_version_code
    gradle(task: "bundleRelease")
    upload_to_play_store(track: "beta")
  end

  lane :release do
    increment_version_code
    gradle(task: "bundleRelease")
    upload_to_play_store(track: "production")
  end
end

# Usage:
# fastlane ios beta
# fastlane android beta

sections.title: Troubleshooting Common Issues
sections.description: Solutions to the most common React Native development problems.

items.label: Issue 1: Metro Bundler Errors
items.text:
Symptoms: Bundling fails, red screen with syntax error, module not found.

Solutions:
1. Clear Metro cache: npx react-native start --reset-cache
2. Clear npm cache: npm cache clean --force
3. Delete node_modules and reinstall: rm -rf node_modules && npm install
4. Check import paths for typos and case-sensitivity
5. Ensure all dependencies are installed
6. Check for duplicate modules (npm dedupe)
7. Restart the bundler completely

items.label: Issue 2: iOS Build Fails
items.text:
Symptoms: Build error in Xcode, pod install fails, missing frameworks.

Solutions:
1. cd ios && pod install --repo-update
2. Clear derived data:
   rm -rf ~/Library/Developer/Xcode/DerivedData
3. Check Xcode version compatibility
4. Update CocoaPods: sudo gem update cocoapods
5. Check iOS deployment target matches pod requirements
6. Open .xcworkspace (not .xcodeproj)
7. Clean build folder in Xcode (Cmd+Shift+K)

items.label: Issue 3: Android Build Fails
items.text:
Symptoms: Gradle build error, dex issues, SDK not found.

Solutions:
1. cd android && ./gradlew clean
2. Check Java version (JDK 17+ required)
3. Check Android Gradle Plugin version compatibility
4. Update Android SDK in Android Studio SDK Manager
5. Enable multidex if method count exceeds 64K
6. Check build.gradle for dependency version conflicts
7. ./gradlew --stop (kill all Gradle daemons)
8. Check local.properties for SDK path

items.label: Issue 4: App Crashes on Launch
items.text:
Symptoms: App opens briefly then closes, no error message.

Solutions:
1. Check native crash logs: 
   iOS: open Xcode -> Window -> Devices and Simulators -> View Device Logs
   Android: adb logcat *:E
2. Check for unlinked native modules
3. Disable Hermes temporarily to compare
4. Check Sentry or other crash reporting tools
5. Test in Release mode vs Debug mode
6. Check for null pointer exceptions
7. Verify all third-party SDKs are correctly initialized

items.label: Issue 5: Animation Performance Issues
items.text:
Symptoms: Animations stutter, drop frames, feel laggy.

Solutions:
1. Use useNativeDriver: true for Animated API
2. Use Reanimated for UI-thread animations
3. Avoid creating new Animated.Value on every render
4. Use LayoutAnimation for layout changes
5. Reduce bridge crossing during animations
6. Enable Hermes engine
7. Check for heavy JS thread load
8. Use InteractionManager for non-critical work

items.label: Issue 6: Push Notifications Not Working
items.text:
Symptoms: Notifications not received, permissions not showing.

Solutions:
1. iOS: Ensure Push Notification capability is enabled in Xcode
2. iOS: Check APNs certificates are valid
3. Android: Check Firebase Cloud Messaging setup
4. Verify device token is being sent to backend
5. Test with physical device (not simulator)
6. Check notification channel settings (Android)
7. Verify notification content (title, body not empty)
8. Check app is not in foreground (or implement foreground handler)

sections.title: Summary & Next Steps
sections.description: Key takeaways and resources for continuing your React Native journey.

items.label: What You've Learned
text: Architecture: Bridge vs JSI/Fabric/TurboModules — Understanding the New Architecture
Setup: Expo (managed/dev client) vs React Native CLI (bare workflow) — choosing the right approach
Project Structure: Feature-based organization for scalability and maintainability
Core Components: View, Text, Image, TextInput, ScrollView, FlatList, Pressable and their native mappings
Styling: StyleSheet, Platform-specific styles, NativeWind/Tailwind CSS, and the Box Model differences from web
Flexbox/Yoga: Flex direction, justify/align content, flex wrap, gap, and common layout patterns
Navigation: React Navigation with Stack, Bottom Tabs, Drawer, type-safe navigation params
State Management: Zustand (recommended), Redux Toolkit, Jotai, Context — when to use each
Networking: fetch, Axios interceptors, TanStack Query for server state caching and offline support
Data Persistence: AsyncStorage, MMKV (high-performance), WatermelonDB for complex data
Device APIs: Camera, location, biometrics, permissions system, and essential native device features
Lists & Performance: FlatList virtualization, getItemLayout, React.memo, windowSize optimization
Animations: Animated API, Reanimated 4 (UI thread), LayoutAnimation, Lottie — choosing the right tool
Gestures: Gesture Handler with Pan, Pinch, Tap, Rotation, and composed gestures
Forms: TextInput with KeyboardAvoidingView, React Hook Form + Zod validation
Debugging: Flipper, React DevTools, LogBox, Hermes debugger, performance profiling
Testing: Jest for unit tests, React Native Testing Library for component tests, Detox for E2E
Push Notifications: Expo Notifications for managed, Firebase Cloud Messaging for bare workflow
Publishing: Building AAB/APK for Android, Archives for iOS, App Store and Play Store submission
TypeScript: Typed props, generic components, utility types, path aliases
Security: Encrypted storage, certificate pinning, authentication best practices, code obfuscation
CI/CD: GitHub Actions, Fastlane for automated build and deployment

items.label: Next Steps
items.text:
Congratulations on completing the React Native A–Z guide! Here's where to go next:

1. Build a real app — Apply what you've learned by building a complete app from scratch
2. Explore the ecosystem — Learn about Expo Router (file-based routing), Tamagui (UI library), Solito (universal navigation)
3. Deep dive into native modules — Write your own TurboModule or Fabric component using C++/JSI
4. Learn state management patterns — Master Zustand, explore Redux Toolkit for enterprise apps
5. Master the New Architecture — Understand the full JSI/Fabric/TurboModules pipeline
6. Study production patterns — Code push, feature flags, A/B testing, analytics, monitoring
7. Contribute to open source — React Native, Expo, and community libraries welcome contributions
8. Read the official docs — https://reactnative.dev/docs
9. Join the community — React Native Discord, Reddit r/reactnative, Twitter/X #ReactNative
10. Stay updated — Follow the React Native blog, release notes, and RFCs

rows: Expo SDK, EAS Build, development builds
